headline: ECB's Lane: The European economy continues to grow.
ECB's Lane: The US is not a dominant factor in global trade.
ECB’s Lane: The ECB will decide meeting by meeting and be data-dependent. The ECB's job is more reactive at the moment.
ECB's Lane: We'll guide inflation back to target in the next year or so.
ECB's Zigman: We will take stock of situtation in September
ECB’s Makhlouf: Will have more information in September
ECB’s Makhlouf: Inflationary pressures have not gone away
ECB's Rehn: Energy crisis is not yet over
ECB's Rehn: No sign of second-round effects
ECB's Rehn: Need to keep a cool head
ECB's Simkus: We do not see second-round effects of higher inflation
ECB's Simkus: Inflation seen higher than target for a long time
ECB's Simkus: Still see probability of rate hike higher than hold
ECB's Simkus: In September, again we'll have additional inflation data
ECB's Simkus: No value in rushing with a decision now
ECB's Simkus: Inflationary environment has increased
ECB's Simkus: $100 oil will have repercussions on inflation
ECB's Simkus: Past six weeks show uncertainty of the environment
ECB's Moulin: Trump's tariffs add more uncertainty to the world economy and economic growth
ECB's Moulin: Will look at size and duration of oil shock
ECB's Kocher: Growth forecast isn't great, but don't see a recession
ECB's Kocher: ECB will act if inflation outlook deteriorates
ECB's Kocher: I don't see any hard evidence of second-round effects
ECB's Kocher: ECB is in a position to be vigilant for the next couple of weeks
ECB's Kocher: Recent developments in oil markets are concerning
ECB’s Nagel: central bank well-positioned to track upcoming developments closely
BOJ's focus shifts to how firms pass rising costs to households, sources say
ECB's President Lagarde ends press conference
ECB's President Lagarde: Medium-term inflation expectations firmly anchored.
ECB's President Lagarde: Euro enlargement is not for ECB to decide
ECB's President Lagarde: Mild scenario currently looks quite unlikely.
ECB's President Lagarde: The situation in the Middle East can reverse very quickly
ECB's President Lagarde: We did not discuss minimum reserve requirement. Doesn't mean it won't be discussed.
ECB's President Lagarde: No push for ECB rate hike today
ECB's President Lagarde: We asked staff to study various oil and gas prices scenarios for September meeting.
ECB's President Lagarde: Recent developments in the Gulf are alarming.
ECB's President Lagarde: We're back to the baseline.
ECB's President Lagarde: We're providing framework guidance
ECB's President Lagarde: Not giving any forward guidance
ECB's President Lagarde: Seeing gradual decline in wage growth
ECB's President Lagarde: We are not seeing any second-round effects.
ECB's President Lagarde: We will have a lot more data available in September.
ECB's President Lagarde: There were some who asked whether we should consider a hike
ECB's President Lagarde: We are positioned adequately to wait.
ECB's President Lagarde, on risk assessment: We're back to June settings
ECB's President Lagarde: Post-MoU drop in Brent crude was quicker than anticipated
ECB's President Lagarde: We have had some relatively benign developments since the June decision
ECB's President Lagarde: Financial conditions slightly tighter vs last meeting.
ECB's Lagarde: Extreme weather events and climate crisis could drive up food prices.
ECB's President Lagarde: Longer energy prices stay high, the more likely to have second round impacts.
ECB's President Lagarde: Energy shock could intensify further
ECB's President Lagarde: Risks to inflation tilted to upside.
ECB's President Lagarde: Higher energy prices will weigh on real incomes.
ECB's President Lagarde: Risks to growth are tilted to downside
ECB's President Lagarde: Conflict is a major source of uncertainty
ECB's President Lagarde: Energy inflation likely to keep inflation well above target into first half of 2027. Inflation will then decline.
ECB's President Lagarde: Rising labour productivity helps contain unit labour cost growth
ECB's President Lagarde: Most measures of longer-term inflation expectations stand at around 2%.
ECB's President Lagarde: Survyes indicate moderate wage growth.
ECB's President Lagarde: Underlying inflation contained, full effect yet to play out
ECB's President Lagarde: Energy shock feeding into higher prices.
ECB's President Lagarde: Fiscal responses to energy shock should be targeted, tailored and temporary.
ECB's President Lagarde: Urgent action is needed to strengthen the Euro-area economy
ECB's President Lagarde: Drivers of medium-term growth remain intact
ECB's President Lagarde: Growth to remain modest in the near term
ECB's President Lagarde: Firms, households expect labour market to remain weaker than before conflict.
ECB's President Lagarde: Higher defense spending is supporting manufacturing
ECB's President Lagarde: Manufacturing has held up
ECB's President Lagarde: Digital services robust, partly on AI.
ECB's Lagarde: Activity in services partly recovered
ECB's President Lagarde: Recent data points to some improvement in economic activitiy.

ECB: In particular, ECB’s interest rate decisions will be based on its assessment of the inflation outlook and risks surrounding it, in light of incoming economic and financial data, as well as dynamics of underlying inflation
ECB's Lending Survey: Euro zone banks tightened credit standards in Q2 on geopolitical, energy price concerns.
ECB's SAFE Survey: Middle East war forcing firms to seek alternative suppliers, improve energy efficiency.
ECB's SAFE Survey: Firms expect lower increases 'in selling prices, non-labour input costs and wage expectations.
ECB's SAFE Survey: Inflation expectations at the one, three and five-year horizons remained largely stable.
Fed's Hammack: Hearing from businesses who say they think we need to take action to curb inflation, and from consumers who can’t make ends meet about a growing sense of despair.
Fed's Hammack: I’m seeing good growth numbers and stable consumer spending.
Fed's Hammack: Business leaders point to energy costs and supply chain disruptions as factors, but also cite pressures from insurance and the AI data center buildup.
Fed's Hammack: At the same time, I’m seeing good growth numbers and stable consumer spending.
Fed's Hammack: I consider the data as well as conversations I’ve had with business and community leaders in the 4th Fed District.
Fed's Hammack: I’m hearing from conversations with business and community leaders that inflation isn’t coming from only one source—it’s broad-based.
Fed's Hammack: Persistently high inflation is the bigger concern.
Fed's Hammack: There is no conflict in our mandate - LinkedIn Post
Fed's Hammack: Our PCE nowcast shows core inflation at 3.3% after incorporating this week’s data.
Fed's Hammack: Inflation is too high. The labor market is right around my level of maximum employment.
Fed's Hammack: Inflation is too high and broad-based while growth and consumer spending remain solid; the labor market is near maximum employment, making persistent inflation the bigger concern.
Fed's Jefferson: Policy is well positioned to respond to incoming data; energy, trade and AI shocks may affect output and inflation, and repeated shocks could destabilize expectations.

Fed's Logan: Wages are not currently inflationary, but AI investment and data-center electricity demand add near-term price pressure; the balance sheet should be efficient rather than target a fixed size.
Fed's Schmid: Inflation is too hot, persistent and broad-based; recent data are encouraging but insufficient, while the labor market is roughly stable.
Fed's Logan: Inflation is not returning sustainably to 2% on its own, employment downside risks have faded, and modestly higher rates would better balance the outlook.
BoE's Breeden: Absent the war inflation would be at 2%; the Bank remains vigilant to inflation risks.
Fed's Cook: Policy is mildly restrictive and can wait for more data, but she is prepared to act soon if inflation does not slow; risks have shifted toward inflation while jobs remain stable.
ECB's Moulin: ECB needs to be ready for any eventuality on inflation.

Fed's Chair Warsh: Inflation remains unsatisfactory and the Fed may adjust rates and the balance sheet; he favors the smallest practical balance sheet while recognizing AI demand is initially inflationary and productivity later disinflationary.
Fed's Williams: Inflation near 4% is too high but should fall to about 3.25% by year-end and reach 2% in 2028; growth and labor markets are solid, expectations anchored, and policy well positioned.
ECB's Panetta: Euro-zone inflation is around 3% and expected to remain above that level until early 2027; policy must keep expectations anchored and limit second-round effects.
ECB's Nagel: Rates are appropriate after June; react cautiously but decisively if needed.
ECB's Kocher: No second-round effects observed; ECB is ready to act whenever necessary to achieve 2% inflation.

Fed's Goolsbee: June CPI was surprisingly benign and services inflation encouraging, but one month is insufficient; several similar PCE readings would improve confidence while the job market is stable but not strong.

Fed's Chair Warsh: The Fed has no tolerance for persistent inflation and is recommitting to 2%; the labor market is resilient, policy and balance-sheet frameworks will be reviewed, and changes will be communicated in advance.
BoE's Gov. Bailey: Gulf instability clouds the outlook; refined prices have not fallen as much and UK growth is the key issue.

Fed's Waller: Another high core-inflation reading would be a signal that near-term hikes may be needed; labor is stable, inflation credibility remains intact, and he favors a 1.5%-2.5% target range but not changing it now.
Fed's Waller: Balance-sheet reduction depends on reserve demand; he opposes scarce reserves and MBS holdings, while preserving transparency and avoiding rigid forward guidance.
Fed's Chair Warsh: The Fed's dedication to price stability and maximum employment is unwavering and will be pursued rigorously.
BoE's Pill: Productivity has slowed and interest rates will need to rise in the year ahead.
ECB's President Lagarde: Inflation needs to be back at target in roughly three years.

Fed's Williams: Inflation remains far too high and risks lean toward inflation, though labor is stable; policy must respond to energy, tariffs and AI while the balance sheet maintains rate control under ample reserves.
ECB's Dolenc: September forecasts will clarify the outlook; July decision remains uncertain.
ECB's Nagel: Meeting-by-meeting approach is appropriate.
ECB's Escriva: Keep all policy options open on a meeting-by-meeting basis.
RBA's Hunter: Oil shocks may lift inflation expectations; policy should curb indirect and second-round effects and may require higher unemployment to restore low stable inflation.
BoJ's Asada: Not opposed to hikes but wants wage- and demand-backed 2% inflation; oil-cost pass-through is rapid and policy should remain flexible.
Fed's Williams: Policy is in a good place and data-dependent; inflation remains high but lower energy prices help, while growth and the labor market are steady.
ECB's Panetta: Outlook is fragile, with upside inflation and downside growth risks as supply shocks become more frequent.
ECB's Wunsch: Wage developments are benign; another move is possible but significant tightening is not currently required.
Fed's Waller: Labor risks have stabilized while inflation has accelerated; the 2% commitment is credible and forward guidance can help but becomes a hindrance when too rigid.
ECB's Schnabel: The shock cannot simply be looked through; supply-chain pressures remain elevated despite lower oil prices.
ECB's Nagel: Stay vigilant and preserve optionality amid volatile energy conditions.
ECB's Makhlouf: ECB has the absolute will to deliver 2% inflation.
ECB's Moulin: June hike was right, no new hiking cycle was signaled, growth likely remains weak, and September forecasts will guide next steps.
ECB's President Lagarde: June hike was right; supply shock is spreading but second-round effects have not materialised.
BoE's Mann: Inflation persistence and elevated expectations justify a longer hold and potentially an activist move; H2 data are crucial.

Fed's Daly: Policy is slightly restrictive and she favors gradualism; inflation rose with tariffs and oil but labor and consumers remain resilient, leaving both persistent-inflation and weaker-growth scenarios open.
ECB's Stournaras: July likely on hold; monitor indirect war effects on prices and stay at current rates for some time.
ECB's Kaasik: One more rate hike is reasonable; longer-term oil effects are likely and autumn wage data will clarify.

Fed's Chair Warsh: Prices remain too high despite lower inflation risks; the Fed will not tolerate inflation above 2%, avoids forward guidance, prefers rates as the primary tool and a smaller, well-communicated balance sheet.
ECB's Lagarde: Euro area is not in stagflation and risks are more balanced; avoid preset forward guidance.
BoE's Bailey: Rate cuts are off the table; holding reflected a softening economy while policymakers assess energy-price pass-through.
ECB's Kocher: Next decision is hike or hold; inflation threat is lower but not contained and wage deals could keep inflation elevated.
ECB's Nagel: Inflation remains too high and above target into 2027; keep July and September options open.
ECB's Wunsch: One additional hike may suffice without major second-round effects.
ECB's Cipollone: Hold off further hikes until new economic projections.
ECB's Demarco: Additional tightening can wait until projections; no second-round wage or expectation effects warrant haste.
ECB's Kazaks: Urgency for consecutive hikes is lower; no forceful response is currently needed and further hikes may be avoided if conditions improve.
ECB's Dolenc: July pause may be appropriate; if oil stays subdued, decision can wait until September.
ECB's Rehn: Energy shock is stagflationary but expectations remain anchored; decide meeting by meeting without precommitment.
Fed's Hammack: Inflation is broad-based and too high, growth and employment remain solid, and policy shows little restraint; higher rates may be needed to return inflation to target.
BoJ's Sato: Weak yen lifts import costs and lowers real income; firms are raising wages and prices, increasing FX pass-through to inflation.
ECB's Wunsch: Inflation will stay above target for several quarters and another hike may be needed, though not necessarily in July.
ECB's Nagel: Energy shock remains in the system and inflation may stay significantly above target.
ECB's Sleijpen: Lower energy prices will affect inflation; second-round effects remain the key policy risk.
ECB's Lane: Lower oil must pass through the economy; 2027-28 oil prices remain above pre-war levels.
ECB's President Lagarde: War created major inflation pressure; Europe is resilient and ECB can raise rates if needed.
BoE's Pill: Brexit made the UK more prone to self-sustaining inflation and short-to-medium expectations matter more.
Fed's Kashkari: He has one rate hike penciled in for 2026 and rates on hold in 2027; broad inflation and near-term AI investment may require tightening while labor remains decent.

Fed's Williams: Inflation should moderate to about 3.5% this year but reaching 2% is pushed to 2028; growth and labor are resilient, policy is well positioned, and reserve-management purchases can adjust as needed.

Fed's Goolsbee: Inflation is the clear mandate problem, with core and services inflation too high and moving the wrong way; AI-related spending may overheat demand, while rigid forward guidance remains undesirable.
ECB's Schnabel: Further rate hikes are needed to return inflation to 2%; ceasefire is no reason to relax vigilance.
BoJ's Tamura: Underlying inflation has reached 2%; raise rates every few months toward roughly 2% neutral, accelerating or enlarging hikes if overshoot risks rise.
BoJ's Tamura: Opposed pausing bond taper and supports faster normalization of bond holdings; future balance-sheet size should be assessed from liabilities.
BoJ's Governor Ueda: Economy is recovering moderately and conditions remain accommodative; continue raising rates as underlying inflation approaches or overshoots 2%.
RBA's Hauser: Inflation remains far too high; timely policy and prior hikes reduce excessive capacity pressure and may lower unemployment costs.
BoE's Taylor: Weak economy and restrictive policy justify an extended hold; be ready to cut under a benign scenario, with limited second-round effects.
ECB's Lane: Energy shock is broadening inflation; labour market and household balance sheets are resilient, and inflation may stay above 2% into 2027.
ECB's Escriva: Services inflation is persistent and oil is passing through to other sectors amid high uncertainty.
ECB's President Lagarde: Inflation risks are up and growth risks down; no forceful action yet absent de-anchoring or second-round effects.
ECB's Lane: Inflation will remain above 3% this year while the economy retains steady momentum.
BoJ's Himino: Wage-price cycle is becoming entrenched and core inflation nears 2%; delaying adjustment risks overshoot, so hikes likely continue based on data.
BoJ's Himino: Bond-taper pause gives investors time to absorb JGBs; policy must consider bank lending and allow market price discovery.
ECB's Lane: A measured response is warranted; hiking makes sense even under a milder scenario while avoiding abrupt rate changes.
BoE's Bailey, Breeden and Lombardelli: Respond quickly if second-round effects strengthen; higher energy prices must not become persistent inflation.
BoE's Dhingra: Risks are skewed up for prices and down for activity.
BoE's Lombardelli: Holding is appropriate while learning more about the shock; demand has not deteriorated rapidly.

Fed's Chair Warsh: The FOMC held rates because no member favored immediate tightening, but price-stability work remains; policy is not broadly restrictive, and the Fed must prevent energy and food shocks from broadening.
Fed's Chair Warsh: The Fed has the capability and commitment to deliver 2% inflation, dropped forward guidance, and will review communications, the dot plot and analytical frameworks by year-end.

Fed's Chair Warsh: Five independent task forces will study communications, the balance sheet and ample-reserves regime, data sources, productivity and jobs, and inflation frameworks; recommendations and possible SEP changes are expected by year-end.
ECB's Simkus: At least one more rate increase is likely to cap inflation expectations.
ECB's Lane: Inflation above target a year ahead requires proactive policy; energy shock will have indirect effects across food, goods and services.
BoJ's Uchida: 2 trillion yen monthly JGB buying can adjust the balance sheet; increase purchases flexibly if yields surge.
BoJ's Uchida: Continue raising rates as economy, prices and financial conditions evolve; delayed adjustment could require larger hikes, but no 50 bp proposal was made.
RBA's Gov. Bullock: Data match expectations but further tightening remains possible; waiting for inflation to ease may be too late.
BoJ's Tamura: Underlying CPI is already aligned with the price-stability target.
BoJ's Asada: Middle East downside risks to output and jobs exceed upside price risks, so policy should respond cautiously.
ECB's Kazimir: Hike cycle is not complete; second-round effects may emerge and the economy can absorb higher rates.
ECB's Moulin: June hike aimed to contain second-round effects; oil is passing to other prices though wages have not yet responded.
ECB's Lagarde: ECB raised rates 25 bps; no preset path, inflation returns to target in 2H 2027, with upside inflation and downside growth risks.
Fed's Hammack: Holding rates steady is reasonable for now, but action may soon be appropriate if inflation does not cool; the labor market is roughly balanced.
BoE's Dhingra: Future rate decisions depend on energy prices; balance between job weakness and price pressure remains uncertain.
Fed's Schmid: Inflation is the economy's biggest risk; the Fed must decide between patience and rate hikes, keep the 2% message clear, and avoid assuming the oil shock is transitory.
Fed's Daly: Tariffs and oil are driving current inflation while broad AI productivity effects are not yet visible; restoring price stability is the priority without unnecessarily harming the economy.
Fed's Daly: Policy is in a good place and should remain data-dependent without forward guidance; inflation is the top concern while the labor market is resilient and stable.
RBA's Gov. Bullock: Inflation-unemployment trade-off is worsening, though a wage-price spiral is not currently a concern.
Fed's Logan: Inflation is taking too long to return to 2%, policy looks neutral or loose, and mildly restrictive settings or higher rates may be needed later this year; growth and labor remain solid.

Fed's Williams: Policy is modestly restrictive and precisely where it should be, with no current case to raise or lower rates; inflation should peak then decline, although upside risks have increased.
BoJ's Governor Ueda: Basic stance is continued rate hikes; oil broadens price pressure, wage growth is near 5%, and upside inflation risks are increasing.
BoE's Greene: Acting sooner may be preferable; rate hikes may be needed as conflict persists and second-round effects strengthen.
Fed's Hammack: Rates can remain steady for now, but the Fed may need to act soon if broad and persistent inflation does not cool; policy may not be tight enough while employment is near full.
BoJ: One participant said bond purchases should eventually slow to about 1.3 trillion yen per month.
RBA's Harper: Inflation persistence remains a significant issue.
Fed's Daly: AI may eventually lift productivity and reduce inflation without mass unemployment, but price stability must be restored without unnecessary economic damage.
Fed's Paulson: Inflation is too high and weighing on the economy, but mildly restrictive policy is well positioned; holding rates allows more data while growth stays modest and employment near full.

Fed's Bowman: Disinflation has stalled; a prolonged Middle East energy shock could broaden inflation and require a policy shift, though a temporary shock can be looked through to avoid unnecessary restraint.

Fed's Schmid: Inflation is too hot and should not be assumed transitory; policy is not very restrictive and may need tighter rates or balance-sheet restraint, while growth and employment remain balanced.
BoE's Bailey: Economic and labour-market softness justify tolerating temporary above-target inflation, unless second-round effects emerge.
Fed's Kashkari: The Iran war clouds the inflation and policy outlook, but it is too early to conclude that near-term rate hikes are required.
Fed's Barkin: AI investment is creating inflation pressure and may be pushing up the neutral interest rate.
BoJ's Ueda: Temporary oil shocks can become persistent; easy financial conditions continue to support activity.
BoJ's Himino: Middle East developments and global inflation concerns will affect the timing of the next rate decision.
BoE's Taylor: Economy is weak and policy already restrictive; hike only if second-round risks become unacceptable, with recession probability 30-40%.
BoJ's Koeda: Core inflation is near 2% and inflation risk is emerging; raise rates at an appropriate pace while normalizing JGB holdings.
BoE's Dhingra: Economy is too weak for a consumption boom and current restrictiveness may be sufficient under Scenario B.
BoE's Mann: Late-2026 inflation could become embedded in 2027 wage deals; labour-market softening is not broad-based.
BoJ's Governor Ueda: GDP is near forecast but Middle East effects have begun; long-term yields are rising rapidly.
RBA's Hunter: Inflation-expectation risk is elevated; oil-cost pass-through may be faster and broader, with firms planning retail price increases.
BoE's Greene: Energy-shock second-round effects may not appear for another year.
BoE's Pill: A prompt but modest rate increase is advantageous; keep rates restrictive and prevent second-round effects from becoming uncontrolled.
BoE's Breeden: Policy cannot wait forever, but action is not necessarily needed in June or July.
BoE's Mann: Inflation-activity trade-off is stark; policy cannot offset the initial energy shock and excessive tightening could amplify financial conditions.
BoE's Greene: Inflation risks are skewed to the upside.
BoE's Pill: Policy should respond more promptly to new inflation pressure.
BoJ report: Higher oil, weaker yen and lower stocks could reduce FY2026-28 real GDP by 0.1-0.2 percentage point versus baseline.
BoJ's Governor Ueda: Underlying inflation is slightly below 2% but should reach 2% in H2 FY2026; continue raising rates if the economy avoids a major slowdown.
BoJ's Takata: Price-stability goal is largely met and inflation risks tilt upward from secondary effects of overseas price increases.
BoJ's Tamura: With price risks heavily upward, keep the policy rate moving toward neutral.
